title: Digest-sized zine layout
subtitle: Designing and folding an 8-page zine from a single sheet
excerpt: A digest-sized zine is made from a single A4 sheet of paper, folded and cut once to produce an 8-page booklet. The page order follows a specific sequence that only works in one direction: understanding the imposition before you start means you do not draw page 3 where page 7 should go. This tutorial covers the layout, imposition, and fold-and-cut construction.
sourceNotes: Single-sheet 8-page zine imposition is synthesised from established zine-making practice. The format is widely documented in DIY publishing communities.

glossaryTerms.definition: The arrangement of pages on a printed or drawn sheet so that they are in the correct reading order after the sheet is folded and cut. Getting the imposition right before drawing is the only technical requirement of the single-sheet zine.
A half-A4 booklet format (148 × 105 mm when closed), named after the digest-sized US pulp magazines of the mid-20th century. The standard zine format.

content.text: and requires exactly one cut. Fold the sheet, cut once along the centre fold, refold, and a booklet with a cover and seven interior pages appears. The challenge is drawing the pages in the right positions on the unfolded sheet, which means working through the

Fold a spare A4 sheet into the 8-page booklet first. Number the pages 1 through 8 in reading order. Open the sheet flat. Note which page number is in which position on the unfolded sheet, this is the imposition map. Page 1 (front cover) and page 8 (back cover) sit side by side on the front face. The inside pages are arranged in the reverse order across the back face. Drawing on the unfolded sheet without working this out first is the most common source of zine construction errors.

Work on a flat A4 sheet, referencing your imposition map. Each page area is half of half of the A4 sheet, approximately 105 × 74 mm. Use a faint pencil grid to keep the page areas consistent. Some pages will be upside down relative to others on the unfolded sheet; this is correct and expected. Draw all artwork within the page boundaries. Bleed content (design that runs to the edge) requires cutting after folding.

Fold the sheet in half lengthwise (hot-dog fold), then fold in half again across the width. Score the fold lines with the bone folder before folding to get a crisp edge. Open back to the first fold only. Locate the centre fold: this is the line you cut. Cut along this centre fold from the folded edge to the centre of the sheet, a cut about 105 mm long. Refold and the booklet forms automatically. Crease all the spine folds firmly with the bone folder.
